title: The Dark Night of the Soul in Christian Mysticism: St. John of the Cross and Kundalini
meta_description: Hey. Yeah, you. I know you're probably reading this at some ungodly hour, heart hammering, mind racing, feeling like you've fallen down a well and no one e
body:
You're Not Alone in the Dark

Hey. Yeah, you. I know you're probably reading this at some ungodly hour, heart hammering, mind racing, feeling like you've fallen down a well and no one even knows you're gone. I've been there. I've been in that exact spot, staring at the ceiling, wondering if I was losing my mind, if I'd broken something in my brain that couldn't be fixed. The terror is real. The isolation is real. But listen to me - you're not alone. What you're going through, this intensity, this feeling of being utterly undone - it's got names. It's been happening to people for centuries, across cultures, across belief systems. You're not crazy, you're just cracking open. And that's terrifying, I know. I really do.

I'm not here to tell you it's easy. It's not. It's the hardest thing you'll ever go through. But I'll tell you what, it's also the most important. You're being remade, whether you asked for it or not. And right now, it feels like destruction. Like the world's ending inside your head. But it's not the end. It's a beginning. A brutal, agonizing, incredibly confusing beginning. I get it. I really do. I lived through my own kundalini crisis, and let me tell you, it was a dark night that stretched on for years. Years of thinking I was going insane, years of physical pain, emotional torment, spiritual confusion. The kind of stuff that makes you question everything you ever thought was real about yourself, about reality itself. And honestly, it almost broke me. Almost. But I'm here. And you will be too. You're strong enough, even if you don't feel it right now. You wouldn't be going through this if you weren't. Right?

St. John of the Cross: The OG of the Dark Night

So, you're in the thick of it, feeling like your soul is being put through a shredder. You might not even have heard the phrase "Dark Night of the Soul" before this. But it's a thing. A very old thing. And one of the best guides through it, believe it or not, is a Spanish mystic from the 16th century named St. John of the Cross. He didn't have words like "kundalini" or "energy crisis," but he described the experience of spiritual purification with an accuracy that'll make your hair stand on end. He wrote about it in his book, The Dark Night of the Soul by St. John of the Cross (paid link). You should read it. It won't magically fix things, but it'll make you feel seen. It'll make you realize that someone, centuries ago, felt this exact same gut-wrenching despair and confusion.

He talked about two dark nights: the Dark Night of the Senses and the Dark Night of the Spirit. They're not separate things, they often blend and overlap, but it's a useful way to think about what's happening. The first one, the Dark Night of the Senses, is when all your usual spiritual comforts dry up. Prayer feels meaningless. Meditation feels like a torture. You don't feel God's presence, if you ever did. You feel abandoned, empty, numb. All the practices that once gave you solace, they just don't work anymore. It's like God unplugged the spiritual charger. Your old ways of connecting, your old ways of understanding, they're just gone. Poof. And you're left flailing. Know what I mean? It's disorienting as hell. You might even feel like you're losing your faith, or that you never had it to begin with. That's part of the process. It's stripping away all the external props, all the things you thought were spiritual, but were really just crutches.

The Dark Night of the Spirit: The Big One

Then there's the Dark Night of the Spirit. This is the one that really messes with you. This is where your very identity gets challenged. Everything you thought you were, everything you identified with - your ego, your beliefs, your attachments, your sense of self - it all starts to dissolve. It's like your entire internal world is being vaporized. And it's terrifying. Truly terrifying. You feel like you're disappearing, like you're losing your mind, like you're dying. And in a way, you are. The old you is dying. Your old way of perceiving reality, your old operating system, it's crashing. Hard. St. John described it as a purification, a cleansing. He said God is doing this to burn away all the impurities, all the things that keep you from truly seeing, truly being. It's incredibly painful because you're fighting it every step of the way. You're clinging to the old, familiar self, even though it's causing you immense suffering. But you don't know any other way to be. Does that land?

This isn't just an emotional or psychological thing, either. It's a deep, deep spiritual process that hits you on every level. You might feel a sense of dread, despair, a feeling of meaninglessness that's so deep it makes you want to curl up and cease to exist. You might experience intense anxiety, panic attacks, depression that feels like a physical weight. Your sleep might be screwed up. Your appetite might disappear. Your body might feel strange, buzzing, vibrating, like it's not even yours anymore. These are all signs that something much bigger than just "feeling sad" is happening. This is a complete overhaul, from the inside out.

Kundalini: The Energy Awakening

Now, let's talk about kundalini. Because for many of us, what St. John described, that spiritual purification, it's intimately tied to an energy awakening. Kundalini is often depicted as a coiled serpent at the base of the spine. When it "awakens," it's said to ascend through the chakras, purifying them, transforming your consciousness. Sounds great on paper, right? Like some spiritual superpower. But the reality for many, myself included, is anything but serene. It's a freaking energetic hurricane. It's not a gentle awakening, it's an explosion. And it can feel incredibly destabilizing.

When kundalini activates, especially spontaneously or without proper guidance, it can trigger a whole host of symptoms that mirror or intensify the Dark Night experience. You get those intense internal sensations: heat, cold, vibrations, tingling, pressure, electrical currents running through your body. You might have involuntary movements, tremors, muscle spasms. Your nervous system goes into overdrive. You might hear sounds, see lights, have visions. Your mind can become incredibly active, racing thoughts, intense dreams, vivid nightmares. Old traumas, buried emotions, unresolved issues - they all get dredged up to the surface, demanding to be processed. It's like your entire energetic and psychological closet is being emptied out, all at once. And it's messy. So, so messy. People talk about "kundalini awakening" like it's some blissful thing, but for many, it's a crisis. A full-blown, terrifying crisis. Look, I've seen it. I've lived it. It's not for the faint of heart.

I remember lying in bed, feeling like my body was vibrating so hard it was going to shake apart. My mind was going a million miles an hour, replaying every mistake I'd ever made, every fear I had. I couldn't sleep. I couldn't eat. I couldn't connect with people. I felt like I was literally being electrocuted from the inside. I thought I was developing some rare neurological disorder, or that I was just losing my marbles. Doctors just looked at me blankly. Therapists didn't know what to do. That's why I'm telling you this. Because if you're experiencing anything like that, you're not alone, and you're not making it up. It's real. And there are resources. The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) is a classic that documents many of these symptoms. It can be validating just to read that others have gone through similar hell.

The Overlap: Purification and Energy

Here's the thing: St. John of the Cross didn't use the word "kundalini," but his descriptions of spiritual purification, of the burning away of the ego, the intense emotional and psychological distress, the feeling of utter abandonment - they fit so perfectly with what many experience during an intense kundalini activation. It's like two different maps for the same terrifying territory. The Dark Night is the spiritual purification process, and for many, kundalini is the energetic engine driving that purification. It's the fire that burns away the dross, as the mystics would say. And it's not a gentle flame; it's a roaring inferno.

The kundalini process forces you to confront everything you've suppressed. Every trauma, every shadow, every untruth you've told yourself. It strips away your illusions, your attachments, your false sense of security. It leaves you feeling raw, exposed, vulnerable. And that's exactly what St. John described as the work of the Dark Night - God stripping away everything that isn't truly you, everything that keeps you from ultimate union. It's a painful process of disillusionment, of seeing reality as it truly is, without all your preconceived notions and desires clouding your vision. And when your energy system is firing on all cylinders, when kundalini is active, that process gets turbo-charged. It can feel like your nervous system is being rewired, and in a way, it is. Your entire being is being reconfigured for a higher state of consciousness. But the transition? It's hell. No sugarcoating it. It's absolute hell.

Survival Strategies for the Abyss

So, you're in the abyss. What do you do? I'm not going to give you platitudes. I'm going to give you what I learned the hard way. What worked for me when I thought I was going to crack. Because you can get through this. You really can.

  • Acceptance, not resistance: This is the hardest one, I know. Your natural instinct is to fight it, to make it stop, to go back to "normal." But the more you resist, the more intense it often becomes. Try, just try, to lean into it. To say, "Okay, this is happening." It's not about liking it, it's about acknowledging it. Surrender is a terrifying word, but it's often the only way through. You're not giving up, you're giving in to a process that's already underway.
  • Grounding practices: When your energy is flying, you need to ground yourself. This means connecting to the physical world, to your body. Simple things. Walking barefoot on the earth. Spending time in nature, even if it's just looking out a window. Eating nourishing, simple food. Taking warm baths. Gentle movement, like slow stretching or walking. Anything that brings you back into your physical form and out of the spinning chaos of your mind.
  • Simple routine: Your old life might feel impossible. Don't try to force it. Create a simple, basic routine. Wake up, drink water, maybe a short walk, eat a meal, rest. Don't push yourself to do anything more than the bare minimum. Your energy is being used for a massive internal transformation. Conserve it.
  • Limit stimulation: Turn off the news. Limit social media. Don't watch scary movies or listen to jarring music. Your nervous system is already overloaded. Be incredibly gentle with what you allow into your awareness.
  • Seek support: This is crucial. But be discerning. Not everyone will understand. Find people who have been through something similar, or who are open-minded enough to listen without judgment. A good therapist who understands spiritual emergence can be a lifesaver. Look into resources like the Spiritual Emergence Network (SEN) or other organizations that specialize in this. You might find The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) helpful, as it also discusses the importance of seeking appropriate support.
  • Don't try to "fix" it: You can't fix this with your mind. It's not a problem to be solved, it's a process to be endured. Trying to intellectualize it, trying to figure it all out, will just make your mind race more. Let go of the need to understand everything right now. The understanding will come, eventually.
  • Journaling: Even if it's just stream-of-consciousness, getting the chaos out of your head and onto paper can help. It's a way to witness what's happening without getting completely consumed by it.
  • Patience, immense patience: This isn't a weekend retreat. This is a marathon through hell. It takes time. Sometimes a lot of time. Be patient with yourself. There will be good days and bad days. Don't judge your progress. Just keep putting one foot in front of the other.

The Other Side: What's There?

So, you're probably wondering, what's on the other side of this nightmare? Is there even an "other side"? Yes. There is. It doesn't mean all your problems disappear, or that life becomes a perpetual state of bliss. That's not how it works. But what you gain is invaluable. You gain a depth of understanding, a clarity of vision, and a connection to something much bigger than yourself that you couldn't have imagined before. You're stripped down to your essence. All the false layers are gone. You're left with pure, unadulterated you. And that's incredibly powerful.

You'll find that your capacity for compassion, for empathy, for love, expands exponentially. You'll see the world with new eyes. Your priorities will shift. What once seemed important will seem trivial, and what you once overlooked will become deeply meaningful. You'll have a resilience you never knew you possessed. Because if you can get through this, you can get through anything. You'll know, deep in your bones, that you're connected to something eternal, something unbreakable. And that knowledge, that direct experience, is worth all the pain. It really is. It’s not just a philosophical idea anymore; it’s a lived reality. This journey, as terrifying as it is, leads to a deep spiritual maturity. It's a direct path to knowing yourself, truly knowing yourself, in a way most people never do. It's not an easy path, but it's a real one. The Far Side of Madness by John Weir Perry (paid link) explores how these intense inner experiences, often labeled as "madness," can actually be a process of inner healing and transformation. It's a validating read, helping you see the potential for growth in what feels like utter breakdown.

So yeah, you're scared. You're confused. You feel like you're losing it. I know. I've been there. But hold on. Just hold on. Breathe. Take it one moment at a time. This isn't the end. It's just the darkest part of the journey. And the dawn will come. It always does. You're stronger than you think. You're going to get through this. I promise you.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
